title: Chapter 19: The Flanking Fire
content: The flanking force came through the service tunnels, just as Orsi had planned. Three Viscounts leading thirty Barons through passages so narrow they had to crawl single file, through ventilation shafts that hadn't been used in centuries, through the forgotten arteries of a city that had grown around its own defenses and forgotten they existed.

Marcus was the first to hear them. Twelve years of living in Greyhaven, of mapping every tunnel and passage, of memorizing the routes that even the oldest vampires had forgotten — it all came flooding back when he heard the scrape of claws on ancient stone. He turned from the Thorn Gate, where the Polaris Guard was still holding the line, and saw the first Viscount emerge from a collapsed ventilation shaft thirty feet behind their position.
"Rear guard!" he shouted. "Flanking attack, three o'clock!"

Ten soldiers broke from the formation. Sera was with them — she'd been fighting at Marcus's side all morning, her golden veins blazing with every swing of her blessed blade. The transformation had made her faster than any human, stronger than any paladin. The Viscount who emerged from the shaft took one look at her — at the luminescent veins, the golden eyes, the white-hot fury of a woman who'd spent months becoming something terrible — and hesitated.
It was the last mistake he ever made.

Sera's blade took his head before he could raise his claws. The silver-blessed steel cut through vampire flesh like sunlight through shadow. The Viscount's body crumpled, and the Barons behind him — thirty of them, still climbing out of the narrow shaft — found themselves facing a paladin who wasn't quite human anymore and ten Polaris Guard soldiers who'd been training for exactly this scenario.
"Silver formation!" Marcus commanded, and the soldiers fell into a defensive crescent around the shaft entrance, their crossbows aimed at the emerging Barons, their stakes ready for close combat.
"Hold them here," Sera said. She was already moving toward the second shaft, where another Viscount was pulling himself into view. "I'll handle the leaders."
"Sera, wait—"
But she was gone, a blur of golden light and blessed steel, and the Viscount who'd been about to order his Barons forward suddenly found himself very busy defending his own neck.
The battle at the flanking point lasted twenty minutes. It felt like twenty years.

Marcus's soldiers held the crescent formation against wave after wave of Barons. Silver bolts sang through the darkness. Stakes found hearts and throats and eyes. The tunnel floor grew slick with vampire blood — dark and thick, pooling in the ancient stone grooves. Three of the Polaris Guard fell. Marcus dragged one of them back from the line himself, his silver eyes blazing with a fury that had nothing to do with hunger and everything to do with twelve years of accumulated hatred.
"No one else dies today," he growled, driving a stake through a Baron's chest. "No one."

Sera killed both remaining Viscounts. The first fell to a blade through the heart — a clean strike, textbook paladin technique. The second died harder, his claws raking across her shoulder before she drove her sword up through his jaw and into his brain. She felt the wounds heal almost instantly, the Pact flooding her with Kael's power, her golden veins flaring brighter with every injury she survived.

When it was over, thirty Barons lay dead in the service tunnel, three Viscounts were ash on the stone floor, and Sera was standing in the carnage with blood on her hands and light in her eyes and an expression that was neither human nor vampire but something entirely new.
"Marcus," she said, her voice strangely calm. "Status?"
"Three wounded. No deaths. Flanking force neutralized." He looked at her — at the blood and the light and the terrible serenity in her face. "You okay?"

"I don't know." She looked at her hands. The golden veins were brighter than they'd ever been, pulsing with a rhythm that matched Kael's heartbeat through the Pact. "I think I just killed three Viscounts. I think I should feel something about that."
"What do you feel?"
"Hungry." She met his eyes. "And ready for more."

At the Thorn Gate, Kael felt the flanking attack end through the Pact — felt Sera's wounds heal, felt her fury spike and then settle into something colder and more controlled. He allowed himself exactly three seconds of relief. Then he turned his attention back to the battle in front of him.

The coalition's main force had broken. The Barons who'd survived the initial assault had fled back into the eastern tunnels. The Viscounts had retreated to Orsi's position, their soldiers routed, their strategy in ashes. The Thorn Gate was secure. Greyhaven had held.
But the battle wasn't over.
In the darkness beyond the gate, Count Orsi's golden eyes watched through the scrying mirror, and his cold smile never wavered.
Because the flanking force had been a distraction. The real attack was still coming. And it was coming from the one direction Kael hadn't thought to defend.
